channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Immigration Hard-Liners Repeatedly Lost in Court Before Justices Ruled in Their Favor
item.description: “This is a victory 10 years in the making,” a White House official said after the Supreme Court ruled that the Trump administration could end deportation protections for some migrants.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:description: Stephen Miller, the White House deputy chief of staff, is an architect of some of President Trump’s most aggressive immigration policies,

item.title: Justices Clash on Whether Race Played a Role in Trump’s Bid to Deport Haitians
item.description: The split mirrored one that has long divided Americans: how seriously to take the president’s loose, provocative and sometimes ugly remarks.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:description: Justice Samuel A. Alito Jr.’s majority opinion did not include any examples of President Trump’s statements about Haitians.

item.title: Here’s What the Supreme Court’s Decision Means for TPS Holders
item.description: The ruling on Temporary Protection Status will have swift repercussions for migrants and employers alike.
item.dc:creator: Jazmine Ulloa, Lydia DePillis and Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Haitian family receives help obtaining passports and documents at the St. Vincent de Paul center in Springfield, Ohio, this year.

item.title: Big Wins for Trump at the Supreme Court, and a $2.5 Billion Cyberattack Whodunit
item.description: Plus, the Friday news quiz.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Jazmine Ulloa and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: A loose collective of cybercriminals initially took credit for crippling Jaguar Land Rover last year. Investigators now see Russian hands behind the ransomware attack.

item.title: How Three Months of War Changed the Gulf Forever
item.description: Facing an emerging U.S.-Iran deal that leaves them vulnerable, Gulf Arab countries are reshaping their defense strategies, economies and trade routes.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Omanis around Qaboos Port in Muscat, where oil tankers, carriers, vessels and Omani fishing boats were sitting anchored this month.

item.title: After Attacking Cargo Ship, Iran Presses Its Claim to Authority Over Strait
item.description: Iran issued a statement on Friday asserting its right to control traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, a day after firing on a container ship that took a route near Oman’s shore.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Oil tankers and cargo vessels anchored off the coast of Oman this week.

item.title: Stocks in Asia Tumble as A.I. Uncertainty Jolts Tech Shares
item.description: Crude prices are again approaching prewar levels, as trading remains volatile.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: The Aftermath of Venezuela’s Earthquakes
item.description: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez, a photographer for The New York Times, describes the scene on the ground after the Venezuelan earthquakes.
item.dc:creator: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez, Leila Medina and Estelle Caswell

item.title: Hoping for Miracles as Venezuela Digs Out From Massive Quakes
item.description: It is known as a “doublet,” two earthquakes in quick succession, and it has brought the Latin American country to its knees.
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín, Anatoly Kurmanaev, Julie Turkewitz, Isayen Herrera and Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Looking for survivors on Thursday in the rubble of a building in Catia La Mar, Venezuela, that collapsed a day earlier in an earthquake.

item.title: He Was Deported Back to Venezuela and Started Anew. Then the Quakes Hit.
item.description: Venezuelans in South Florida were frantically trying to track down friends and relatives after two earthquakes in Venezuela. Jorge Erazo was among the missing.
item.dc:creator: David Ovalle
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Civilians and emergency medical workers looking for survivors in the rubble of a building that collapsed during an earthquake in Catia La Mar, Venezuela.

item.title: Intel’s Chip Business Shows Signs of Life After Years of Struggle
item.description: It is the centerpiece of President Trump’s drive to make more chips in the United States, but the company still has a long way to go before it can be called a complete turnaround.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle

item.title: The High School Pipeline to South Korea’s Chip-Making Fortunes
item.description: Huge memory-chip profits from the global A.I. boom have increased interest in semiconductor factory work. But behind the hype are uncertain job prospects.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.media:credit: Tina Hsu for The New York Times
item.media:description: A class about diffusion equipment operation taught by Kang Soo Jin.

item.title: How a Niche Technology Became a Choke Point for A.I.
item.description: Advanced chip packaging, which boosts computing power for artificial intelligence, has made the United States more reliant on Taiwan than ever.
item.dc:creator: Don Clark and Gabriela Bhaskar
item.media:description: A silicon wafer reflecting Subramanian Iyer, a specialist at the University of California, Los Angeles, in a technology called advanced chip packaging.

item.title: Mamdani’s Rent Freeze Is Approved by New York City Board
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s pledge to freeze rents for rent-stabilized apartments became a centerpiece of his campaign. Six months into his first year, a city panel delivered.
item.dc:creator: Mihir Zaveri
item.media:credit: Madison Swart for The New York Times
item.media:description: As New York City’s housing crisis has intensified, tenant advocates have pushed for a rent freeze, while landlords have argued it would result in more poorly maintained buildings.

item.title: Europe’s Deadly Heat Wave: A Jolt for Climate Action, or Just for A.C.?
item.description: Heat-related deaths and disruptions to daily life are forcing politicians to reckon, in different ways, with a rapidly warming planet.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear and Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Carrying a floor fan during the heat in Paris on Thursday.

item.title: Europe’s Scorching Heat Is Gradually Moving East
item.description: Parts of Spain, France and other countries are seeing slight relief, but the record-breaking heat wave is far from over.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel

item.title: Heat Wave Prompts Paris to Suspend Sports Events and Public Drinking
item.description: The Paris police chief, Patrice Faure, said that alcohol restrictions were intended to ease pressure on the health services.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: Paris is temporarily barring alcohol consumption in streets and parks.

item.title: Climate Change Fueling Europe’s Ferocious Heat Wave, Scientists Find
item.description: A scientific analysis concluded that such high temperatures, across so much of the continent, would “not have been possible” without global warming.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: A shady passageway on Tuesday in Bordeaux, France, where temperatures reached 108 degrees Fahrenheit, or roughly 42 Celsius.

item.title: Pro-MAGA Groups Spur Trump Push For Cultural Change In Schools
item.description: One nonprofit, Defending Education, initiated nearly a dozen civil rights investigations targeting diversity programs and transgender policies.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nicole Neily, the president and founder of the conservative parents’ rights group Defending Education, near her home in Arlington, Va.

item.title: How Trump’s Education Department Is Targeting Trans Students
item.description: Our Washington correspondent Michael Bender explains how the Trump administration is trying to use a civil rights law that prohibits sex-based discrimination to roll back protections for transgender students.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender, Nikolay Nikolov, Paul Abowd, Jon Miller, Rafaela Balster, Stephanie Swart and Ray Whitehouse

item.title: Obama Says He Occupies a ‘Suite’ in Trump’s Head
item.description: Former President Barack Obama says President Trump has an obsession with him and that Mr. Trump “knows better” than to say “crazy stuff” to his face.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.category: Obama, Barack H, Presidential Library and Museum
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former President Barack Obama at the opening of the Obama Presidential Center in Chicago last week.

item.title: John Bolton, Former Trump Adviser, Is Expected to Plead Guilty in Classified Information Case
item.description: Mr. Bolton is expected to admit to mishandling classified information and could face time in prison, in an inquiry that spanned the Trump and Biden administrations.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi and Devlin Barrett
item.category: The Room Where It Happened: A White House Memoir (Book)
item.media:description: John Bolton, a former adviser to President Trump, is expected to plead guilty to mishandling classified information on Friday.

item.title: After Almost 50 Years, Etan Patz’s Father Is Relieved to Have Closure
item.description: In a rare interview, Stanley Patz said he hoped that a decision reinstating the conviction of Pedro Hernandez would end the ordeal of going to court and reliving the day of his son’s disappearance.
item.dc:creator: Hurubie Meko

item.title: Is There a Founding Story That Can Unify Left and Right?
item.description: There has never been agreement over the meaning of America’s creation 250 years ago. Maybe there shouldn’t be.
item.dc:creator: Jia Lynn Yang
item.media:credit: Kean Collection/Archive Photos, via Getty Images

item.title: A $2.5 Billion Whodunit: The Hack That Dented the U.K. Economy
item.description: A loose collective of cybercriminals initially took credit for crippling Jaguar Land Rover last year. Investigators now see Russian hands behind the ransomware attack.
item.dc:creator: Adam Goldman, Jane Bradley, Dustin Volz and Michael Schwirtz
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Land Rovers being transported from the Jaguar Land Rover factory in Solihull, England, last year.

item.title: With ‘Supergirl,’ Milly Alcock is Learning to Accept the Fear
item.description: After “House of the Dragon,” a starring role in another big franchise, “Supergirl,” gave Milly Alcock pause. It’s “this new gift of learning to accept the fear.”
item.dc:creator: Clarissa Cruz
item.media:credit: Molly Matalon for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Supergirl’ Review: This Glass Ceiling Is Made of Kryptonite
item.description: Milly Alcock is naturally appealing in this derivative, if altogether watchable, superhero movie.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: The Supreme Court Is Unshackling the Presidency
item.description: Where is Chief Justice John Roberts? And why isn’t he putting limits on this administration?
item.dc:creator: Kate Shaw

item.title: Is It Time for a New Sexual Revolution?
item.description: One that actually brings men and women together.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat and Sophia Alvarez Boyd

item.title: Protected No More
item.description: We look at the impact of the Supreme Court’s immigration rulings.
item.dc:creator: Tom Wright-Piersanti

item.title: Late Night Grades the Attractions at the Great American State Fair
item.description: The fair has everything, Jimmy Fallon said, including “a Trump approval rating roller coaster, which has the biggest drop in history.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: Zelensky Steps Up Threats Against Belarus for Aiding Drone Attacks
item.description: Ukrainian officials say their northern neighbor is allowing its radio relay stations to be used to guide Russian attack drones more precisely.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall and Oleksandr Chubko
item.media:description: A destroyed bridge over the Dnipro River, which forms the border between Ukraine and Belarus, in Chernihiv region, Ukraine, this month.
